coredata.dc:title: Ultrasonic-assisted recycling of Nile tilapia fish scale biowaste into low-cost nano-hydroxyapatite: Ultrasonic-assisted adsorption for Hg2+ removal from aqueous solution followed by “turn-off” fluorescent sensor based on Hg2+-graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sricharoen, Phitchan
Limchoowong, Nunticha
Nuengmatcha, Prawit
Chanthai, Saksit
coredata.dc:description: This study was planned to recycle calcium and the phosphorus-rich Nile tilapia fish scale biowaste into nano-hydroxyapatite (FHAP), using ultrasonic-assisted extraction of calcium and phosphorus from fish scales, which was optimized in term of extraction time, acid concentration, extraction temperature, and ultrasonic power. These two elements were determined simultaneously by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry and the FHAP phase was formed upon addition of the extracted element solution in alkaline medium using homogenous precipitation assisted with ultrasound energy. The FHAP adsorbent was characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller. A combination of FHAP and the ultrasonic method was then used to remove Hg2+ from aqueous solution. Four significant variables affecting Hg2+ removal, namely, adsorbent dosage, pH, ultrasonic power, and adsorption time, were studied. The results exhibited that the optimal conditions for maximizing the removal of Hg2+ were 0.02 g adsorbent dosage, pH 8, 0.4 kW ultrasonic power, 20 min adsorption time, and 30 °C adsorption temperature. The sorption mechanism of Hg2+ was revealed by isotherm modeling, indicating that FHAP adsorbent has a potential for Hg2+ removal in aqueous media with the maximum adsorption capacity being 227.27 mg g−1. This adsorption behavior is in agreement with the Langmuir model as reflected by a satisfactory R2 value of 0.9967, when the kinetics data were fitted with pseudo-second-order. Therefore, the FHAP could be an alternative adsorbent for the ultrasonic-assisted removal of Hg2+ at very high efficiency and within a very short period of time.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasonic-assisted extraction of Ca and P from Nile tilapia fish scales towards hydroxyapatite
Procedure for ultrasonic-assisted adsorption of Hg2+
UAE optimization of Ca and P from fish scales toward hydroxyapatite synthesis
Scanning electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS)
Evaluation of the influence of different parameters on the adsorptive performance of FHAP for the adsorption of Hg2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Post-Doctoral Program from Research Affairs and Graduate School, Khon Kaen University ( 60162 ). The authors also thank Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Khon Kaen University, Thailand for all the support. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of FHAP at pH 8–12.
SEM images of FHAP at pH 8–12.
EDS spectra of FHAP in precipitates prepared at pH 8–12.
TEM micrographs of FHAP at pH 8–12.

Effect of the pH on Hg2+ adsorption. Conditions: Hg2+ concentration, 40 mg L−1; sample volume, 10 mL; adsorption temperature, 30 °C; adsorbent dosage, 0.02 g; adsorption time, 30 min; ultrasonic power, 0.4 kW.

Effect of adsorbent dosage on Hg2+ adsorption. Conditions: Hg2+ concentration, 40 mg L−1; sample volume, 10 mL; adsorption temperature, 30 °C; pH, 8; adsorption time, 30 min; ultrasonic power, 0.4 kW.

Effect of ultrasonic power on Hg2+ adsorption. Conditions: Hg2+ concentration, 40 mg L−1; sample volume, 10 mL; adsorption temperature, 30 °C; adsorbent dosage, 0.02 g; pH, 8; adsorption time, 30 min.

Effect of ultrasonic time on Hg2+ adsorption. Conditions: Hg2+ concentration, 40 mg L−1; sample volume, 10 mL; adsorption temperature, 30 °C; adsorbent dosage, 0.02 g; pH, 8; ultrasonic power, 0.4 kW.
EDS spectra of adsorbent before (a) and after (b) the adsorption of Hg2+ complex on FHAP.
Pseudo-first (a) and pseudo-second order models (b) for Hg2+ adsorption onto FHAP.
Performance of the FHAP nanosorbents in terms of reusability.
Isotherm constants for the adsorption of Hg2+ onto FHAP.
Comparison of sorption capacity of Hg2+ by various adsorbents.
Kinetics parameters of FHAP for Hg2+.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aInitial Hg2+ concentration was 40 mg L−1 .

head.ce:title: Ultrasonic-assisted recycling of Nile tilapia fish scale biowaste into low-cost nano-hydroxyapatite: Ultrasonic-assisted adsorption for Hg2+ removal from aqueous solution followed by “turn-off” fluorescent sensor based on Hg2+-graphene quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: 123 Mittraphab Road, M. 16, T. Ni-Muang, A. Muang, Khon Kaen University, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kean 40002, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study was planned to recycle calcium and the phosphorus-rich Nile tilapia fish scale biowaste into nano-hydroxyapatite (FHAP), using ultrasonic-assisted extraction of calcium and phosphorus from fish scales, which was optimized in term of extraction time, acid concentration, extraction temperature, and ultrasonic power. These two elements were determined simultaneously by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry and the FHAP phase was formed upon addition of the extracted element solution in alkaline medium using homogenous precipitation assisted with ultrasound energy. The FHAP adsorbent was characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller. A combination of FHAP and the ultrasonic method was then used to remove Hg2+ from aqueous solution. Four significant variables affecting Hg2+ removal, namely, adsorbent dosage, pH, ultrasonic power, and adsorption time, were studied. The results exhibited that the optimal conditions for maximizing the removal of Hg2+ were 0.02 g adsorbent dosage, pH 8, 0.4 kW ultrasonic power, 20 min adsorption time, and 30 °C adsorption temperature. The sorption mechanism of Hg2+ was revealed by isotherm modeling, indicating that FHAP adsorbent has a potential for Hg2+ removal in aqueous media with the maximum adsorption capacity being 227.27 mg g−1. This adsorption behavior is in agreement with the Langmuir model as reflected by a satisfactory R2 value of 0.9967, when the kinetics data were fitted with pseudo-second-order. Therefore, the FHAP could be an alternative adsorbent for the ultrasonic-assisted removal of Hg2+ at very high efficiency and within a very short period of time.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has applications in all divisions of science and technology with diverse substances being prepared and used for the elimination of water pollutants and numerous attempts being made to discuss various aspects of water treatment by adsorption using nano-adsorbent materials [1]. The nanomaterial-based sorbent is an excellent solid phase due to its demonstrated ability to elevate, to a great extent, the sorption capacities of the target analytes. This is because of a special feature of nano-scaled materials, i.e., high surface-area-to-volume ratio, making it a promising solid sorbent for adsorption procedures and homogeneous distribution in solution. Fish is consumed worldwide on a day-to-day basis contributing toward a huge amount of fish biowaste, comprising of fish scales. This may leave residues in the environment leading to health problems and pollution if not properly treated or utilized. Fish scales have a potential for reuse as raw material or could be converted into valuable products, which could be an effective measure toward waste management of fish biowaste, resulting in a lower environmental impact. Over the past several years, fish scales which are rich in calcium and phosphorus, have been used for the synthesis of calcium hydroxide phosphate (IUPAC name) or hydroxyapatite (HAP, Ca10 (OH)2 (PO4)6) by several methods. HAP has been extensively studied due to its practical applications and natural occurrence and is used as an artificial bone substitute in orthopedic, neuro, dental, and plastic surgeries [2]. In addition, HAP and its composite can also be utilized as an alternative adsorbent [3,4]. HAP is prepared by precipitation methods using calcium nitrate (Ca (NO3)2, potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), and ammonia (NH3) [5] or by using phosphoric acid (H3PO4) solution with calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) [6]. The development of alternative methods toward eco-friendly experimentation has greatly progressed. For example, the preparation of HAP from Labeo rohita fish scales was performed by Modal et al. [7]. They used an alkaline heat treatment method with very high-temperature thermal treatment at 700–800 °C for calcination and a temperature of 1200 °C for sintering to get the HAP. Paul et al. [8] also synthesized nano-crystalline HAP from Catla catla fish scales using calcination at 200 °C, 400 °C, 800 °C, 1000 °C, and 1200 °C to produce biogenic HAP powder. Pon-On et al. [9] produced HAP material from fresh Probarbus jullieni scales by alkaline hydrolysis using the magnetic stirrer method, while Kongsri et al. [10] employed the alkaline heat treatment method using a magnetic stirrer device to extract the HAP from Tilapia nilotica fish scales. Similarly, Sathiskumar et al. [11] used the fish scales of Labeo rohita to synthesize HAP by alkaline-heat method followed by calcination. However, HAP production using the magnetic stirrer assisted method requires a long period for completion. Huang et al. [12] employed the process of enzymatic hydrolysis with tilapia fish scales for obtaining HAP. However, it is quite expensive due to the chemicals used for enzymatic hydrolysis and the process is also difficult to scale up. It is widely known that ultrasound waves are powerful in accelerating various steps such as homogenization and mass transfer followed by acoustic cavitation. This emerges from the growth and collapse of micro-bubbles and generation of final acoustic streaming induced by sound waves creating microscopic turbulence through the solid particles. It also increases the dispersion of the material leading to lower consumption of the adsorbent, which could result in high removal efficiency and equilibrium in a very short time [1,13,14]. Water pollution and water impurities along with the depletion of water resources are serious environmental threats faced by us today. Heavy metals are the basic and most important pollutants which are widely distributed in air, water, soil and foods released via different operations [15]. Mercury is one of the most toxic heavy metals due to its bioaccumulation and is highly toxic even at very low dosages causing a serious threat to the ecological system as well as human health [16]. For example, Minamata and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) diseases are neurological syndromes involving the neurons responsible for controlling muscle movement [17]. Mercury ions tend to accumulate in living organisms and are known to be carcinogenic [18]. Therefore, the removal of mercury species and controlling these compounds is an essential task.

To the best of our knowledge, although an extensive amount of information is at hand concerning the synthesis/preparation of HAP from fish scales (FHAP), no ultrasonic method has yet been applied for this purpose. Among a variety of approaches, the utilization of ultrasound for material synthesis has been extensively examined over many years and is now positioned as one of the most powerful tools in nanostructured material synthesis [19]. For these reasons, alternatively, we present a simple, fast, green and efficient way to synthesize FHAP using ultrasonic technology. Elements like calcium and phosphorus present in fish scales have an important role in FHAP production. Hence, firstly the concentration of elements in Nile tilapia fish scale derived FHAP was increased and the ultrasonic-assisted extraction (UAE) conditions of Ca and P in the sample, such as extraction time, acid concentration, temperature, and ultrasonic power were optimized. The UAE was carried out under the synergistic effect of ultrasound action, vibration as well as heating, which significantly improved extraction efficiency, enhanced sample throughput, and accelerated the process of extraction [20]. Then the FHAP powders synthesized at different pH with a suitable ultrasonic power were characterized by XRD, SEM, EDS, TEM, FTIR, and BET while ICP-AES was used for the determination of Ca and P in the extracted solutions. The next step in our work is to use FHAP as an adsorbent for Hg2+ adsorption in ultrasonic-assisted batch experiments. Since FHAP material is low-cost, found in natural source, and does not produce toxic hazardous waste, and the Hg2+ is highly toxic and its removal from water is of the utmost importance. So, we believe that this study involves a good adsorbent used for Hg2+ adsorption, and because thus far there have only been a limited number of studies, it will make a valuable and original contribution to the literature. Different conditions (initial pH, adsorbent dosage, and ultrasonic power) were optimized to obtain the best performance of FHAP for Hg2+ removal from aqueous solution and the adsorption isotherm and kinetics of Hg2+ were analyzed.

All the chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. Calcium nitrate, ammonium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium acetate, sodium bicarbonate, monosodium phosphate, sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium carbonate and sodium hydroxide were purchased from QRec™ (New Zealand). Mercury nitrate, diammonium hydrogen phosphate and calcium nitrate tetrahydrate were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Hydrochloric acid (UNILAB, Australia), acetic acid (UNILAB, Australia) and sodium chloride (APS, Australia) were used.
Nile tilapia fish scales were washed carefully by water. Then, the scales have been dried at 60 °C for a day. Dried fish scales were kept in a desiccator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonic-assisted extraction of Ca and P from Nile tilapia fish scales towards hydroxyapatite
ce:section.ce:para: Five gram of the cleaned dried fish scales was weighed in a beaker, 0.8 M HCl 100 mL was mixed with the scales. Then, UAE were performed using a 40 kHz ultrasonic bath (VGT-2300B, GT SONIC, China) at 0.4 kw of ultrasonic power, 60 °C of temperature and 45 min of time. The supernatant was collected and pH 12 with NaOH was adjusted. After that, it was sonicated at 0.4 kw and room temperature for 30 min, filtered, then the granules were cleaned with DI water several times, dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h and finally grinded with a mortar. FHAP were finally achieved prior to use as an adsorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for ultrasonic-assisted adsorption of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: An adsorption experiment was performed in test tube containing 10 mL of Hg2+ solution in power and temperature controlled ultrasonic bath. A 0.02 g adsorbent in 10 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 8) containing Hg2+, was placed on an ultrasound bath and sonicated at 0.4 kw for 20 min. Next, the Hg2+-adsorbent was centrifuged and the supernatant was collected for Hg2+ determination by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fluorescent sensor.

In the field of fluorescent sensors, GQDs are generally used as a selective probe for the determination of Hg2+ because of its simple, fast and non-toxic method [21]. It defined as zero-dimensional carbon-based material that are typically single or few-layer graphenes with lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm [22]. Synthesis of GQDs were synthesized from citric acid via pyrolysis process [23–27]. A 0.2 g citric acid was positioned in a round bottom flask and heated at 200 °C on an oil bath (5 min). The citric acid was slowly melted to liquid and turned yellow color. Then, NaOH solution (0.25 M, 20 mL) was mixed with the liquid at room temperature under continuous stirring for 30 min. The achieved GQDs was stored at 4 °C until use.

Supernatant sample 1 mL was mixed with 75 µL GQDs solution and 4 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 7.4 (final concentration of GQDs is 150 mg L−1). The solution was analyzed using a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). The Hg2+ quenched fluorescent spectra of GQDs were recorded at λex/λem 370/460 nm with 5 nm of excitation and emission slit widths. The spectral results were used for plotting the quenching calibration graph for Hg2+. An amount of equilibrium adsorption, qe (mg g−1), by FHAP was obtained by the subsequent equations [28]:
(1)
q e
=
C 0
-
C e
× V / m
where C0 is initial adsorbate concentrations (mg L−1) and Ce is its final. V is volume of solution (L) and W is weight of the adsorbent (g).

Langmuir (Eq. (2)) and Freundlich (Eq. (3)) models were the most frequently used to define the adsorption isotherms [29,30]:
(2)
q e
=
q
max
k l
c e
/ 1 +
k l
c e
(3)
q e
=
k f
c e
1 / n
where qmax (mg g−1) is the maximum adsorption, kl (L mg−1) is Langmuir adsorption equilibrium constant, kf (mg1–1/n L1/n g−1) is Freundlich constant and n is intensity of adsorption.

The kinetic experiments were performed by the similar conditions as explained in 2.3. except for suspensions were sonicated at fixed-time intervals (5–120 min). Pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order equations were used to fit kinetic experimental data [31]:
(4)
Pseudo - first
order : l o g
q e
-
q t
= l o g
q e
-
k 1
/ 2.303
t
(5)
pseudo - second
order : t /
q t
= 1 /
k 2
q
e 2
- t /
q e
where, qe is adsorption capacity at equilibrium (mg g−1), qt is the adsorption capacity (mg g–1) at various times, t (h). k1 is the rate constants of pseudo-first order (h−1) and k2 is the rate constants of pseudo-second order (g mg–1 h−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UAE optimization of Ca and P from fish scales toward hydroxyapatite synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The simultaneous determination of Ca and P by ICP-AES (Perkin–Elmer OPTIMA 2100 DV ICP-AES, Wellesley, Massachusetts, USA) was performed. The operational system was controlled using the PE Winlab software. Table S1 shows all the conditions used for ICP-AES.

For the extraction of Ca and P, 5 g of dried fish scales and 100 mL of acid solution were fixed to study the extraction performance of the UAE under different experimental conditions. All the experiments were performed in three replicates.

In the UAE process, extraction time was varied as 15 min, 30 min, 45 min, 60 min, 75 min, and 90 min as shown in Fig. S1. Ca and P were found to increase with an increase in time and remained almost constant after 45 min. Therefore, 45 min was selected as the optimum extraction time.

Dilute HCl is broadly used in extraction for element determination [32]. Therefore, various HCl concentrations (0.2–1.2 M) were examined. Fig. S2 shows that the amount of elements increased with increasing HCl concentration up to 0.8 M, and stayed constant thereafter. Consequently, HCl (0.8 M) was chosen as the optimum acid concentration.

The effect of temperature on the solvent extraction of Ca and P with HCl diluted in deionized (DI) water was studied and the extraction temperature was tested between 30 °C and 60 °C. The results obtained are summarized in Fig. S3. It was shown that the simultaneous quantitative extraction of Ca and P increased with increasing temperature and the best results could be obtained with an extraction temperature greater than 60 °C. From the results, it can be concluded that the optimum temperature for Ca and P is 60 °C and resulted in a yield of 396.44 and 164.43 g kg−1, respectively.

High ultrasonic power increases bubble formation that facilitates extraction. The samples were sonicated at different powers such as 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 kW, with keeping time and temperature constant. The result from Fig. S4 indicates that the extraction efficiency for both Ca and P increased with the increase in power up to 0.4 kW. Therefore, 0.4 kW could be considered as the optimum ultrasonic power.

The XRD patterns of the powdered solids were recorded with monochromatic Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm), using a speed of 4 min−1 and 2θ range of 20–50° (model: PANalytical, Netherlands). After Ca and P extraction from fish scales, the pH of the sample solution was adjusted to cause the co-precipitation reaction of hydroxyapatite. In order to control pH during the preparation, the pH was varied from 8 to 12 by adding NaOH dropwise and the synthesis was ultrasonically accelerated. Moreover, the chemical synthesis of hydroxyapatite was also performed using a reaction between calcium nitrate and ammonium dihydrogen phosphate [33] by a co-precipitation method for the comparison with the proposed method. As can be observed in the XRD patterns (Fig. 1 ), the characteristic diffraction signals at 25.98°, 31.69°, 39.56°, and 46.59° were assigned to typical peaks of HAP [34], which clearly explained that the main configuration of the fish scales was HAP. The observed diffraction peaks matched well with the standard JCPDS 00-009-0432 (HAP) which has the (0 0 2), (1 0 2), (2 1 0), (2 1 1), (3 0 0), (2 0 2), (3 1 0), (3 1 1), (1 1 3), (2 0 3), (2 2 2), (3 1 2) and (2 1 3) planes [35,36]. The higher peak intensity of FHAP at pH 12 compared to other pH values concorded with the increasing content of HAP in fish scales. Thus, the pH was adjusted to 12 in the experiment. The XRD pattern of FHAP was quite similar to that obtained by chemical synthesis. It reveals that the ultrasonic-assisted co-precipitation technique is a suitable method to produce FHAP particles from Nile tilapia fish scale biowaste.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS)
ce:section.ce:para: SEM observations coupled with EDS analysis were performed on a 1450VP Scanning Electron Microscope (Zeiss (LEO), England). The effect of pH control during co-precipitation was studied on the morphology. During precipitation, NaOH was added drop-wise to the reaction mixture to adjust the pH. Fig. 2 summarizes the observations of the effect of pH control and aging at pH 8 to pH 12 on FHAP morphology. In the SEM images, some plates could be observed when pH was adjusted to 8 during the process. Upon adjusting pH to 9, the main feature was particles with a small part being plates. Several plates disappeared when pH was increased above 9 during precipitation and small particles were formed. In Fig. 3 , EDS studies of FHAP in precipitates prepared at pH 8 to pH 12 showed that all the particles contained calcium (Ca), phosphorus (P), oxygen (O), carbon (C) and gold (Au), demonstrating the successful deposition of hydroxyapatite. However, the EDS spectra showed the presence of Au because the gold-coated sample was used in the electron microscopy field to increase sample imaging. The values of Ca/P ratio at pH 8 to pH 12 experiments were 1.44, 1.58, 1.58, 1.65 and 1.68, respectively. As the objective of this research was to synthesize FHAP powders with a Ca/P ratio equal to 1.67 [37,38], which is close to the stoichiometric ratio of natural hydroxyapatite. Hence, it can be concluded that the optimum pH for the production of FHAP is pH 12 with 1.68 Ca/P molar ratio.

TEM images for synthesized FHAP powders from pH 8 to pH 12 are presented in Fig. 4 . It can be observed that the morphology, shape, and size of samples changes with varying pH. At pH 8 and 9, the particle shape is unclear indicating inhomogeneous nanostructure, where particles with morphology close to plate-shaped hydroxyapatite showed a high level of aggregation. At pH 10, the sample is composed of elliptical shape with some agglomeration. When pH is changed to 11 and 12, the shape and size are also changed slightly and most of the FHAP particles are observed to be of rice shape. Their sizes were estimated to be around 22.8 ± 2.9 nm in length and 8.6 ± 2.4 nm in diameter using ImageJ software. It was shown that the size of the particles becomes shorter. On the contrary, nanoparticles synthesized at lower pH have more complicated and overlapping shapes.

FTIR spectra of FHAP prepared at pH values of 8–12 are given in Fig. 5 . The stretching band of hydroxyl group (OH−) appears at about 3420 cm−1. Carbonate (CO3 2−) bands between 1577 and 1415 cm−1 represent asymmetric stretching. The CO3 2− results from the ambient CO2 dissolved during the synthesis procedure and FHAP particles are very susceptible to CO2. Peak around 1650 cm−1 is indicative of H2O bending mode. Phosphate (PO4 3−) peaks can be observed at 1091 cm−1, 1044 cm−1 and 1032 cm−1. The vibrations at 877 cm−1 were assigned to bending vibrations of P–O–H for the inorganic Ca – P component [39].

The specific surface area, pore volume and average pore size of the adsorbent were evaluated by the BET method at 77 K of liquid nitrogen and investigated on an Autosorb-1 Quantachrome instrument (USA). The specific surface area, pore-volume, and average pore size of the FHAP were 102.2 m2 g−1, 0.28 cm3 g−1, and 9.14 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the influence of different parameters on the adsorptive performance of FHAP for the adsorption of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The Hg2+-GQDs as the fluorescence sensor is shown in Fig. S5. The quenching of GQDs fluorescent intensity by Hg2+ ion solution was studied at 460 nm (excited at 370 nm). Fluorescent intensity decreased with increasing Hg2+ concentration (Fig. S5(a)). The calibration plot of Hg2+-GQDs was prepared using five concentrations of Hg2+ (0.1–3 mg L−1). The equation of the linear calibration curve was y = −218.63x + 747.07 (y is the FL intensity and x is Hg2+ concentration, mg L−1) with R2 = 0.9953 (Fig. S5(b)).

The pH of heavy metal solutions is a critical parameter significantly affecting the adsorption process and adsorption yield of metal ions from aqueous solutions due to the functional groups associated with adsorbent particles and the adsorbed molecules being affected by H+ ion concentration in the solution [4]. The effect of initial pH for evaluating adsorption efficiency was analyzed by the adsorption capacity, qe (mg g−1) and batch experiments at pH values between 3 and 11 were performed, where the pH at the point of zero charge (pHPZC) of FHAP is 7.74 (Fig. S6). At pH values lower than 7.74, the FHAP surface is positively charged and at pH values higher than 7.74, it is negatively charged. In Fig. 6 , it can be observed that the qe increases with increasing pH values from 3 to 8. When present in solution, the adsorption of Hg2+ is low because of the electrostatic push occurring between Hg2+ ions and the surface binding sites, with the surface of FHAP being positively charged at pH <7.74. In addition, Hg2+ and hydronium ions (H3O+) also compete with the other ions to be adsorbed on the FHAP surface because the solution contains a lot of H3O+ ions. At pH > 7.74, such as pH 8, negative charge is present on the FHAP surface and qe value of Hg2+ increased. Above pH, 8 no effect was observed on the qe values of Hg2+. The main species of Hg2+ is Hg (OH)2 at pH values above 5. However, this is only true if Hg2+ concentration is higher than 120 mg L−1. With an initial Hg2+ concentration of 40 mg L−1, no significant changes in dissolved Hg2+ were found at pH values between 1 and 12. Therefore, with an increase in pH, the qe ascended distinctly and eventually reached equilibrium at pH value around 8.

Fig. 7 shows that with an increase in the adsorbent dosage, the qe decreases. This may be attributed to the decrease in total adsorption surface area available to Hg2+ resulting from aggregation and overlapping of adsorption sites [23]. So, FHAP of 0.02 g was selected as the optimum adsorbent dosage because qe was the highest at this value.

Enhanced adsorption capacity may be due to improved ultrasonic power. The energy of ultrasound may shift the adsorption equilibrium by accelerating mass transfer due to the hydro-dynamical effects created by ultrasound cavitation [40]. Shockwaves and microjets produced by ultrasound cavitation can disrupt adsorbent structure leading to higher qe. The significant and positive effect of ultrasonic power on Hg2+ removal efficiency is shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen that an ultrasonic power of 0.4 kW leads to the best and highest efficiency for the removal of Hg2+.

The effect of adsorption time on the qe of FHAP for Hg2+ was considered. From Fig. 9 , it can be observed that qe increases at the beginning (0 min) until the adsorption goes to the equilibrium state which was achieved within 20 min. From 0 min to 20 min of adsorption time, the adsorption rate of Hg2+ can be described by an increased availability in the number of active sites on the surface of the FHAP adsorbent. Hg2+ adsorption is generally controlled and drastically increased by the process of diffusion from the bulk solution to the adsorbent surface. In the final period, Hg2+ adsorption is likely an attachment controlled step due to fewer available sorption sites. Thus, 20 min was selected as the optimum time for Hg2+ adsorption.

In order to reveal the rate-determining process in Hg2+ adsorption, adsorption kinetics was further examined by testing the effect of contact time on qe of Hg2+. The obtained kinetic models were fitted to pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models. The kinetic parameters were evaluated for 0.02 g of FHAP added to 10 mL of 40 mg L−1 Hg2+ solution at pH 8, with constant (0.4 kW) ultrasonic-assisted adsorption at ambient temperature (30 ± 1 °C). The kinetic parameters for the adsorption of Hg2+ on FHAP adsorbent are shown in Table 3 and Fig. 11 which depicted the qe,cal value of the sorbent to be 20.92 mg g−1 while the R2 is 0.9982. The calculated (qe,cal = 20.92 mg g−1) and experimental (qe,exp = 18.25 mg g−1) values of qe were found to be in close agreement with each other. Consequently, Hg2+ adsorption using FHAP fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model.

One of the significant factors considered while assessing the performance of the sorption materials is the reusability of the FHAP. Thus, the sorption-desorption cycles were repeated several times using the same sorbent. The FHAP was found to be stable during the operation process for six reusable times without significant loss of sorption capacity and the removal was higher than 80% which decreased thereafter (Fig. 12 ). Therefore, the studied Hg2+ was found to be reusable for at least six times.

We demonstrated that the FHAP prepared from cheap and biowaste sources through a green method exhibit the ability to remove Hg2+ from aqueous solutions. Ca and P elements from Nile tilapia fish scales were ultrasonically extracted in acid solution and the FHAP phase was formed upon mixing the extraction phase in alkaline medium using the homogenous precipitation assisted with the ultrasound energy. The characterization of the FHAP synthesized through the ultrasonic process suggested the formation of rice shape nanoparticles (22.8 ± 2.9 nm in length and about 8.6 ± 2.4 nm in diameter). Under optimized conditions, the maximum removal of Hg2+ was achieved at 0.02 g adsorbent dosage, pH 8, 0.4 kW ultrasonic power, 20 min adsorption time, and 30 °C adsorption temperature. The adsorption behavior was fitted to the Langmuir isotherm with extremely high adsorption capacity achieved for Hg2+ (227.27 mg g−1) and kinetic data were best described by a pseudo-second-order model. The excellent adsorption capacity of Hg2+ solution could be ascribed to the ion exchange of Hg2+ to Ca2+ in FHAP structure and the electrostatic interactions between the positively charged Hg2+ and the electronegative FHAP surface. Our work demonstrates the recycling of fish scales biowaste into inexpensive, useful and non-toxic FHAP sorbent with potential for environmental applications, using a fast, green and simple ultrasonic method. Finally, we believe that this FHAP can be applied as an adsorbent for wastewater treatment or it will be extensively investigated for future use in biomedical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Post-Doctoral Program from Research Affairs and Graduate School, Khon Kaen University (60162). The authors also thank Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Khon Kaen University, Thailand for all the support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2020.104966.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
